clues: Although previously fatal before age 4, modern treatments targeting faulty proteins have transformed this into a chronic disease
The nickname 'sixty-five roses' is also the unofficial symbol of the disease
This disease was described in European folkelore, 'the child will soon die whose brow tastes salty when kissed'
Absent in dogs and cats, this was believed to have evolved over 80 million years ago and was first accurately depicted in the early 1500s in drawings by Leonardo da Vinci
Holding high concentrations of lymphoid tissue, this organ has been reported to grow as long as 10.24 inches long
Due to comparisons to a worm, this is frequently described as 'vermiform'
Researchers discovered that this medication had mild effects on neurotransmitters and further research into this medication's structure later aided in the discovery of SSRIs
Although on the BEERS list and side effects are common, this remains a widely used over the counter medication
Known for significant sedation, a typical dose can cause impairment equivalent to a blood-alcohol level of 0.1
A historical precursor derived from the French lilac was used in medieval Europe to treat excessive urination
The chemical name is N,N-dimethyl imido dicarbonimidic diamide and it works partially by activating AMPK in the liver, muscles, and peripheral tissue
On the WHO list of Essential Medications, this was originally marketed in France under the name 'Glucophage'
The fourth leading cause of death worldwide, approximately 6% of the adult population in the US are affected
Recorded as far back as 1679, early descriptors used to describe the affected organs were 'voluminous' or 'turgid'
The Fletcher and Peto curve provided a foundational understanding of the natural history of this disease and the impact of smoking as well as smoking cessation
